shortDescription.text: Missing authorization check on inviter role

fullDescription.text: The invite-email endpoint does not verify the inviter's role before allowing member invitations. Any authenticated and initialized workspace member can invite new members with any non-owner role (admin, editor, normal, dataset_operator). The check_workspace_member_invite_permission function only validates workspace-level policy (enterprise feature), not whether the current user has sufficient privileges (e.g., admin or owner) to perform invitations. A normal or dataset_operator user could invite new admin-level members.

shortDescription.text: Privilege escalation via role assignment in invitation

fullDescription.text: A low-privileged user (e.g., 'normal' or 'dataset_operator') can invite new members with the 'admin' role because the endpoint only checks that the role is a non-owner role via is_non_owner_role(). There is no check ensuring the inviter's own role is at least as privileged as the role being assigned. This allows horizontal and vertical privilege escalation through invitation.

shortDescription.text: Invitation token leaked in API response

fullDescription.text: The invitation token is returned directly in the API response as part of the activation URL. If the inviter's session is compromised or the response is logged/intercepted, the token can be used to activate accounts. The token should ideally be sent only via the invitation email, not returned to the inviter in the HTTP response body.

shortDescription.text: Exception message leakage in error response

fullDescription.text: When invitation fails with a generic Exception, the raw exception message is returned to the client via str(e). This can leak internal implementation details, stack trace fragments, or database error messages to an attacker, aiding further exploitation.

shortDescription.text: Insufficient email input validation before invitation processing

fullDescription.text: The emails list from user input is iterated and passed to RegisterService.invite_new_member() with only .lower() normalization. There is no explicit validation of email format (e.g., RFC 5321 compliance) at the controller level. If the downstream invite_new_member service does not properly validate or sanitize the email, this could lead to email header injection or delivery to unintended recipients. The Pydantic model declares emails as list[str] without email validation constraints.

message.text: The POST /console/api/workspaces/current/members/invite-email endpoint does not check the inviter's role. Any authenticated workspace member can invite new members regardless of their own privilege level. The check_workspace_member_invite_permission() only verifies enterprise workspace-level policy, not the user's role within the workspace.

A user with 'normal' or 'dataset_operator' role can invite new members with the 'admin' role. The only role validation is is_non_owner_role() which permits admin, editor, normal, and dataset_operator. No check compares the inviter's role against the requested invitee role to prevent privilege escalation.

The invitation token generated by RegisterService.invite_new_member() is embedded in a URL and returned in the HTTP response body. This token should only be delivered via the invitation email to limit exposure. Returning it in the response increases the attack surface for token theft.

Generic Exception handler at line 120 returns str(e) directly in the response. Internal error messages may disclose sensitive implementation details such as database schema, file paths, or service internals.

User-supplied email strings from the 'emails' field are passed through with only .lower() normalization. The Pydantic model uses list[str] without EmailStr or regex validation. If downstream services do not validate email format, this could enable email header injection or abuse of the invitation flow for spam.
